entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: ST (Skilled Technical) composite >= 101. This is the anchor figure named in this project's prep research and was cross-corroborated by three independent secondary sources this session (operationmilitarykids.org, cogn-iq.org, battalionduty.com); an official-looking Army fact sheet PDF (api.army.mil/e2/.../35f.pdf) surfaced in search results but was not independently fetched to confirm the exact wording this session -- treat as corroborated-but-not-primary-fetched. A higher score improves competitiveness for a training slot but 101 is the stated minimum.
entry_requirements.education: High school diploma or GED (Army general accession standard). This is an entry-level MOS; no prior military experience required.
entry_requirements.age_range: 17 (with parental consent) to 42, per the Army's 2026 policy ceiling -- data/branch-profiles.json. No 35F-specific age restriction found.
entry_requirements.physical: Standard Army accession medical and fitness standards (Army Fitness Test/AFT); normal color vision is specifically required for this MOS per secondary sourcing this session.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: TS/SCI (Top Secret/Sensitive Compartmented Information) required -- must meet TS/SCI access eligibility, adjudicated via a Single Scope Background Investigation (SSBI)/Tier 5 investigation. An interim clearance lets a Soldier begin MOS training at Fort Huachuca before full adjudication completes; full adjudication must be reached within 12 months of MOS award, per secondary sourcing this session. U.S. citizenship is required.
entry_requirements.other: Under DoD's 13 Adjudicative Guidelines (Security Executive Agent Directive 4 / 32 CFR Part 147), none of the following are automatic mechanical disqualifiers under the 'whole person' standard, but they are the dominant real-world reasons applicants and incumbents are denied or lose eligibility, and each can rule out this specialty even with a fully qualifying ASVAB score: (1) unresolved foreign contacts, foreign relatives in certain countries, foreign property/financial interests, or foreign romantic ties that create potential foreign leverage (Guideline B, Foreign Influence); (2) a pattern of serious financial delinquency -- unresolved debt (especially over $10,000), recent bankruptcy, wage garnishment, or repeated late payment patterns (Guideline F; financial issues are cited as roughly 45% of DoD clearance denials); (3) illegal drug use, misuse of prescription medication, or continued use after applying (Guideline H); and (4), treated especially seriously, deliberately concealing or falsifying any of the above -- including an UNREPORTED dual citizenship or undisclosed foreign contact -- during the application or investigation (Guideline E, Personal Conduct). A 16-24-year-old considering this cluster should treat clearance eligibility as a real, independent requirement, not a formality layered on top of a qualifying ASVAB score.

training_pipeline.location: One of four Army training centers (Fort Jackson SC, Fort Moore GA, Fort Sill OK, or Fort Leonard Wood MO)
training_pipeline.description: Standard Army basic training common to all MOSs. (data/branch-profiles.json)

training_pipeline.location: U.S. Army Military Intelligence Center of Excellence, Fort Huachuca, AZ
training_pipeline.description: All-source intelligence analysis: threat/order-of-battle analysis, the intelligence cycle, briefing and written-product tradecraft, and intelligence systems. The 16-week figure is a moderate-confidence, commonly-cited secondary figure (not a number this session independently confirmed by direct fetch of a primary Army training document) -- treat as approximate and verify against a current TRADOC course catalog before publishing as fact.

pay_and_compensation.methodology_note: Computed, not authoritative: (E-1, <2 YOS basic pay $2,407.20/mo + enlisted BAS $476.95/mo) x 12 = $34,609.80/yr. BAH is intentionally EXCLUDED because it is duty-station-specific and cannot be reduced to one national number (pay-tables.json bah.national_number_warning) -- actual total cash compensation is materially higher once a real BAH figure for the member's duty station is added, often the single largest component. Excludes the DoD TSP contribution (up to 5% of base pay, ~$1,444/yr at max match) and TRICARE's non-cash value, neither of which pay-tables.json could quantify to primary-source confidence this session.
pay_and_compensation.source: data/pay-tables.json (RUN COMPENSATION, this project), citing military.com/benefits/military-pay/charts and navycs.com/charts/2026-military-pay-chart.html (primary DFAS/militarypay.defense.gov pages returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch)

bonuses.conditions: No enlistment or reenlistment bonus figure specific to 35F was confirmed this session. Search results surfaced bonus figures for two SISTER military-intelligence MOSs -- 35P (Cryptologic Linguist / Signals Intelligence Voice Interceptor), reportedly up to $40,000 for qualifying foreign-language proficiency, plus up to $45,000 under a separate Civilian Acquired Skills Bonus for relevant technical certification or trade experience -- but these figures are NOT confirmed to apply to 35F itself, which is a different, non-language-restricted MOS. The official HRC Bonus Chart effective 2025-09-26 (hrc.army.mil/wcmt-api/sites/default/wcmtfiles/2025-09/Bonus%20Chart%20effective%2026%20Sep%202025_1.pdf) surfaced in search results but was not fetched/parsed this session. Bonuses are reset quarterly per Army manning needs and change without notice -- verify current 35F eligibility and amount directly against the current HRC bonus chart or with a recruiter before relying on any number.
bonuses.source: HRC Bonus Chart effective 2025-09-26 (hrc.army.mil, surfaced via search, not fetched/parsed this session)

career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: E-1->E-4 in roughly 2-3 years is the Army-wide norm; E-5 board eligibility typically follows at ~4-6 years of service. No 35F-specific timeline deviation was found this session -- treat as the general Army enlisted pattern, not a sourced 35F-specific figure.
reenlistment_decision_points: End of initial term of service (commonly a 3- or 4-year first contract)
Reinvestigation/reclearance milestones -- an adverse finding at a periodic reinvestigation is itself a real, MOS-specific fork not present in most other career fields
Mid-career (~8-10 years) reenlistment tied to promotion/NCOES completion
The 20-year retention decision (pension cliff -- see retirement block)
advanced_schools_or_certs: Basic/Advanced NCO courses at each promotion requirement (standard Army NCOES, not 35F-specific)
Advanced analytic and DIA/service-school intelligence courses exist for this career management field, but no specific current course list was independently confirmed this session
career_progression.officer_conversion_path: Green-to-Gold (ROTC) or OCS into the Army Military Intelligence officer branch (35A); a warrant-officer intelligence track (350-series) also exists. Exact current designations/prerequisites were not independently verified this session.
career_progression.source: Reasoned from general Army enlisted-career-progression structure and secondary MOS guides; not independently fetched to primary-source confidence this session.

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years -- there is no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension for separating at 19 years or 10 years. Before 20 years, an intelligence specialist keeps every dollar of their own TSP contributions and earnings (always vested), plus DoD's automatic 1%-of-pay TSP contribution and its match (vested after 2 years of service) -- all portable into a civilian 401(k)/IRA. The defined-benefit pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years, per DoD's own language. This applies identically regardless of specialty; intelligence carries no special rule.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service (exact window and multiplier set annually per branch; Army's published active-component rate is 2.5x monthly basic pay for a 4-year additional service obligation). No intelligence-specific continuation-pay rate was identified this session -- treat the general BRS figure as the baseline and verify the current branch amount before relying on it.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. It runs on a points system (1 point/drill, 1 point/AD day, up to 15 membership points/year) toward a 20-'good year' qualifying threshold, with a non-regular pension that generally starts at age 60 (reducible, not below age 50, for qualifying post-2008 mobilized duty). Applies identically to Guard/Reserve intelligence billets as to any other specialty. Note: security clearance sponsorship and drill-status access to classified systems for Guard/Reserve intelligence personnel is a real, separate administrative dependency not captured by the points system itself.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, in exchange for reduced monthly retired pay until full Social Security retirement age (~67), when the monthly amount returns to the uncut level. Fully taxable in the year received.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility (up to 180 days of permissive duty in the final window before separation) is not specialty-restricted, so 35F Soldiers near separation are as eligible as any other MOS in principle -- subject to the same real requirement as everyone else: unit commander approval, which is discretionary based on unit staffing/operational needs (data/transition.json). The cleared-contractor sector (firms supporting DoD/IC customers) is commonly understood to be an active SkillBridge-partner space for this career field, but this was not independently verified this session -- flagged as a plausible inference, not a sourced claim.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, a VA Benefits I course, a DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track (Higher Education, Career/Technical Training, or Entrepreneurship/Boots to Business), ending in a Capstone that certifies Career Readiness Standards are met. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry decides eligibility for nearly every benefit below -- VA disability, VA healthcare, GI Bill, VA home loan, and most state veteran benefits. For this cluster specifically, the DD-214 alone does not carry classification/access information -- a separate record (JPAS/DISS eligibility history) governs whether a departing member's clearance can be reinstated or reused by a future cleared employer.
va_disability_common_claims: GUESS/INFERENCE, not sourced from a specialty-linked VA claims dataset this session (data/transition.json's specialty_linked_common_claims list covers combat arms, aviation, infantry, and PACT Act-covered theater deployment, but does NOT include an intelligence/analysis entry). Reasoned from the nature of the work rather than pulled from a VA claims-frequency source:
Vision strain and related eye conditions from years of sustained close-focus screen/console work reading imagery, signals data, and reporting

Cervical and lumbar spine/musculoskeletal strain from prolonged sedentary console/watch-floor duty (plausible by analogy to other console-heavy specialties, not independently confirmed for this cluster)

Mental-health conditions -- anxiety, depression, occupational stress, and in some cases PTSD-spectrum symptoms -- from routine, repeated exposure to disturbing raw intelligence material (ISR/strike imagery, SIGINT intercepts, HUMINT reporting of atrocities or trauma); this pattern is documented in published research on remote/ISR imagery analysts and drone-associated personnel, but was not independently sourced to a VA claims dataset for this specific cluster this session

For members who deploy forward embedded with a supported combat-arms or aviation unit rather than working from a fixed fusion center, the deployed unit's own exposure profile (noise, PACT Act-covered airborne hazards, etc.) may also apply -- see the combat-arms/aviation/PACT-Act claim families in data/transition.json.
To verify: check VA's published claims-by-occupation data (if VA publishes it) or consult a VSO familiar with intelligence MOS/rating/AFSC populations before treating this list as authoritative.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an employment handicap, filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json), drawn from a separate entitlement bucket than the Post-9/11 GI Bill. No intelligence-specific eligibility variance.

relevant_certifications: No automatic civilian license or certification is conferred by this MOS's training -- see credential_gap_note.
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: CROSSWALK NUANCE (both sides must be named): A current, in-scope TS/SCI clearance is one of the single most valuable transferable assets a service member carries out of the military -- the cleared-contractor labor market (firms supporting DoD/IC customers) pays a real, well-documented premium for candidates who already hold an active clearance, because clearance processing for a new hire can otherwise take many months to over a year. BUT two frictions are equally real and must not be glossed over: (1) a clearance LAPSES after separation -- it does not stay active indefinitely once the member leaves a billet that required it, and reinstating it typically depends on a gap-period rule (commonly cited as roughly 24 months before a full new investigation is required, though this specific window was not independently verified this session) plus a new employer willing to sponsor/re-affiliate the investigation; periodic reinvestigation (every 5 years for SCI access, per the Navy IS sourcing this session) is also required to STAY current while serving. (2) Much of the actual day-to-day work -- specific targets, sources, methods, systems, and products -- cannot be described in any detail on a civilian resume or in an interview, which is a genuine, underdiscussed transition friction: veterans from this cluster often have to learn to translate classified experience into unclassified, resume-safe competency language (analysis tradecraft, briefing/writing skill, tools/platforms where releasable) rather than simply listing what they did. For 35F specifically: all-source analysis skills (synthesizing multiple intelligence disciplines into a single assessment) map fairly directly onto civilian all-source analyst roles at IC agencies and cleared contractors, which is one of the more direct military-to-civilian skill transfers in this cluster -- but the clearance-lapse and resume-opacity frictions above still apply in full.
civilian_crosswalk.source: O*NET 33-3021.06 'Intelligence Analysts' is a detailed occupation nested under the broader BLS Standard Occupational Classification 33-3021 'Detectives and Criminal Investigators' -- BLS's Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OEWS) program does not publish wage data broken out at O*NET's more granular detailed-occupation level, only at the parent SOC code. The range below is therefore the parent SOC 33-3021 10th-90th percentile band (which also includes non-intelligence criminal investigators/detectives, so it is directionally informative rather than an exact intelligence-analyst-only figure). A federal-sector data point for context (not used as the primary range, and not independently verified to primary-source level this session): the federal Intelligence Series (GS-0132) reportedly starts entry-level analysts around GS-7 (~$55,924 in 2024 DC-locality pay per a secondary aggregator), with mid-career GG-0132-13-equivalent postings reported in the $103,409-$134,435 range -- federal/IC pay scales diverge meaningfully from the private cleared-contractor market and from the BLS occupational-category figure used below. https://www.bls.gov/oes/2023/may/oes333021.htm (BLS OEWS, SOC 33-3021 Detectives and Criminal Investigators, May 2024 reference period, $93,580 median / $48,230 10th pctl / $120,460 90th pctl, retrieved via web search this session); https://www.onetonline.org/link/summary/33-3021.06 (O*NET 33-3021.06 Intelligence Analysts detailed-occupation page, confirms the code and typical titles, nested under parent SOC 33-3021); federal GS-0132 context figures via federalpay.org and opm.gov (secondary aggregation, not primary-fetched)

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart / service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. No tier below 50%. Only qualifying Title 10 active-duty days count -- drills, AT, and initial training duty status do not. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment -- a real service commitment, not paperwork-only. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving; cannot be initiated after separation. Dependent children must wait until the member has 10+ years of service to start using it.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting (credit/income/DTI) and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3% of loan amount depending on down payment and first/subsequent use, waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure service history, income, and other VA-benefit receipt -- not by specialty. An intelligence specialist's group assignment depends entirely on their individual disability rating and income profile, not their MOS/rating/AFSC.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property-tax exemptions, income-tax treatment, tuition waivers, etc.) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure -- e.g., a 100%-disabled veteran gets a full residence-homestead property-tax exemption in Texas but a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools (in-state rate applies to all users) or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a housing allowance keyed to the school's zip code and a $1,000/yr book stipend -- scaled by the eligibility tier above. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving. Applies the same across all six branches per data/education-benefits.json; branch-specific semester-hour usage limits were not independently confirmed to primary-source level in that file.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Army Credentialing Assistance (CA)/Army COOL: reduced to a $2,000/fiscal-year cap in 2024, lifetime limit reported at 3 credentials per 10 years of service; commissioned officers reported ineligible. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level for 35F specifically -- cool.osd.mil could not be independently fetched this session (data/education-benefits.json notes a certificate error). (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: No civilian license is conferred automatically by this specialty's military training. The credential that matters most in this labor market is the clearance itself, not a vendor certification. Branch COOL programs may list vendor-neutral analytic-tradecraft or, for cyber-adjacent SIGINT roles, DoD 8140-baseline certifications (e.g., CompTIA Security+) as fundable via Credentialing Assistance -- this was NOT independently confirmed against cool.osd.mil for this specific MOS/rating/AFSC this session (this project's education-benefits.json notes repeated certificate-error failures fetching cool.osd.mil); verify current COOL credential listings for this specific code before publishing a specific certification claim.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: 35F Soldiers deploy embedded with the supported brigade/division's intelligence section (S2/G2) during exercises and combat deployments, and staff garrison intelligence cells between deployments. No sourced deployment-frequency figure specific to 35F was found this session; tempo generally follows the supported unit's operational tempo rather than a rating-specific rotation.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Garrison assignments to fixed intelligence centers can be relatively family-stable; forward-deployed tours follow the supported unit's schedule, which varies widely by unit type and current operational demand.

The applicant AND their immediate family must be U.S. citizens or from a country assessed low-risk under Intelligence Community Directive 704.
entry_requirements.other: Under DoD's 13 Adjudicative Guidelines (Security Executive Agent Directive 4 / 32 CFR Part 147), none of the following are automatic mechanical disqualifiers under the 'whole person' standard, but they are the dominant real-world reasons applicants and incumbents are denied or lose eligibility, and each can rule out this specialty even with a fully qualifying ASVAB score: (1) unresolved foreign contacts, foreign relatives in certain countries, foreign property/financial interests, or foreign romantic ties that create potential foreign leverage (Guideline B, Foreign Influence); (2) a pattern of serious financial delinquency -- unresolved debt (especially over $10,000), recent bankruptcy, wage garnishment, or repeated late payment patterns (Guideline F; financial issues are cited as roughly 45% of DoD clearance denials); (3) illegal drug use, misuse of prescription medication, or continued use after applying (Guideline H); and (4), treated especially seriously, deliberately concealing or falsifying any of the above -- including an UNREPORTED dual citizenship or undisclosed foreign contact -- during the application or investigation (Guideline E, Personal Conduct). For this rating specifically, the ICD 704 family-citizenship requirement is a distinct, additional requirement beyond the general foreign-influence guideline -- a family member's citizenship in a non-low-risk country can be disqualifying independent of the applicant's own conduct.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: CROSSWALK NUANCE (both sides must be named): A current, in-scope TS/SCI clearance is one of the single most valuable transferable assets a service member carries out of the military -- the cleared-contractor labor market (firms supporting DoD/IC customers) pays a real, well-documented premium for candidates who already hold an active clearance, because clearance processing for a new hire can otherwise take many months to over a year. BUT two frictions are equally real and must not be glossed over: (1) a clearance LAPSES after separation -- it does not stay active indefinitely once the member leaves a billet that required it, and reinstating it typically depends on a gap-period rule (commonly cited as roughly 24 months before a full new investigation is required, though this specific window was not independently verified this session) plus a new employer willing to sponsor/re-affiliate the investigation; periodic reinvestigation (every 5 years for SCI access, per the Navy IS sourcing this session) is also required to STAY current while serving. (2) Much of the actual day-to-day work -- specific targets, sources, methods, systems, and products -- cannot be described in any detail on a civilian resume or in an interview, which is a genuine, underdiscussed transition friction: veterans from this cluster often have to learn to translate classified experience into unclassified, resume-safe competency language (analysis tradecraft, briefing/writing skill, tools/platforms where releasable) rather than simply listing what they did. For IS specifically: shipboard and fleet intelligence-support experience (watch-floor tempo, time-sensitive targeting/threat products) is a genuine differentiator for maritime-domain and watch-center civilian roles, but the clearance-lapse and resume-opacity frictions above still apply in full.
civilian_crosswalk.source: O*NET 33-3021.06 'Intelligence Analysts' is a detailed occupation nested under the broader BLS Standard Occupational Classification 33-3021 'Detectives and Criminal Investigators' -- BLS's Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OEWS) program does not publish wage data broken out at O*NET's more granular detailed-occupation level, only at the parent SOC code. The range below is therefore the parent SOC 33-3021 10th-90th percentile band (which also includes non-intelligence criminal investigators/detectives, so it is directionally informative rather than an exact intelligence-analyst-only figure). A federal-sector data point for context (not used as the primary range, and not independently verified to primary-source level this session): the federal Intelligence Series (GS-0132) reportedly starts entry-level analysts around GS-7 (~$55,924 in 2024 DC-locality pay per a secondary aggregator), with mid-career GG-0132-13-equivalent postings reported in the $103,409-$134,435 range -- federal/IC pay scales diverge meaningfully from the private cleared-contractor market and from the BLS occupational-category figure used below. https://www.bls.gov/oes/2023/may/oes333021.htm (BLS OEWS, SOC 33-3021 Detectives and Criminal Investigators, May 2024 reference period, $93,580 median / $48,230 10th pctl / $120,460 90th pctl, retrieved via web search this session); https://www.onetonline.org/link/summary/33-3021.06 (O*NET 33-3021.06 Intelligence Analysts detailed-occupation page, confirms the code and typical titles, nested under parent SOC 33-3021); federal GS-0132 context figures via federalpay.org and opm.gov (secondary aggregation, not primary-fetched)

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart / service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. No tier below 50%. Only qualifying Title 10 active-duty days count -- drills, AT, and initial training duty status do not. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment -- a real service commitment, not paperwork-only. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving; cannot be initiated after separation. Dependent children must wait until the member has 10+ years of service to start using it.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting (credit/income/DTI) and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3% of loan amount depending on down payment and first/subsequent use, waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure service history, income, and other VA-benefit receipt -- not by specialty. An intelligence specialist's group assignment depends entirely on their individual disability rating and income profile, not their MOS/rating/AFSC.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property-tax exemptions, income-tax treatment, tuition waivers, etc.) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure -- e.g., a 100%-disabled veteran gets a full residence-homestead property-tax exemption in Texas but a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: No single named line-score composite located this session (data/interest-taxonomy.json lists MAGE:G as the general cluster composite). CONFIRMED this session via search: a General (G) aptitude score of 64+ is commonly cited as the practical threshold when combined with clearance eligibility; a minimum AFQT score of 65 applies specifically to non-high-school-graduates/non-alternate-credential holders; a minimum score of 20 on the 1N0X1-specific TAPAS/ASVAB Predictive Success Model (PSM) is also required. These figures are search-corroborated, not independently confirmed against a primary CFETP/AFOCD fetch this session.
entry_requirements.education: High school diploma or GED (Air Force general accession standard; no 1N0X1-specific deviation found).
entry_requirements.age_range: 17 (with parental consent) to 42, per data/branch-profiles.json (secondary-reported 2023 increase from 39, not independently confirmed on airforce.com this session).
entry_requirements.physical: Standard Air Force accession medical and fitness standards; no 1N0X1-specific elevated physical standard found. Day-to-day duty is console/analysis-floor based once AFSC-qualified.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: TS/SCI required, or must be submitted for TS/SCI prior to course attendance -- CONFIRMED this session via search. Completion of a current Tier 5 (T5) investigation (per DoDM 5200.02, AFMAN 16-1405) is mandatory; an Airman who cannot obtain at least an interim T5 by the programmed class-start date is not eligible to enter the AFSC.
entry_requirements.other: Under DoD's 13 Adjudicative Guidelines (Security Executive Agent Directive 4 / 32 CFR Part 147), none of the following are automatic mechanical disqualifiers under the 'whole person' standard, but they are the dominant real-world reasons applicants and incumbents are denied or lose eligibility, and each can rule out this specialty even with a fully qualifying ASVAB score: (1) unresolved foreign contacts, foreign relatives in certain countries, foreign property/financial interests, or foreign romantic ties that create potential foreign leverage (Guideline B, Foreign Influence); (2) a pattern of serious financial delinquency -- unresolved debt (especially over $10,000), recent bankruptcy, wage garnishment, or repeated late payment patterns (Guideline F; financial issues are cited as roughly 45% of DoD clearance denials); (3) illegal drug use, misuse of prescription medication, or continued use after applying (Guideline H); and (4), treated especially seriously, deliberately concealing or falsifying any of the above -- including an UNREPORTED dual citizenship or undisclosed foreign contact -- during the application or investigation (Guideline E, Personal Conduct). A 16-24-year-old considering this cluster should treat clearance eligibility as a real, independent requirement, not a formality layered on top of a qualifying ASVAB score.

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years -- there is no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension for separating at 19 years or 10 years. Before 20 years, an intelligence specialist keeps every dollar of their own TSP contributions and earnings (always vested), plus DoD's automatic 1%-of-pay TSP contribution and its match (vested after 2 years of service) -- all portable into a civilian 401(k)/IRA. The defined-benefit pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years, per DoD's own language. This applies identically regardless of specialty; intelligence carries no special rule.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service (exact window and multiplier set annually per branch; Army's published active-component rate is 2.5x monthly basic pay for a 4-year additional service obligation). No intelligence-specific continuation-pay rate was identified this session -- treat the general BRS figure as the baseline and verify the current branch amount before relying on it.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. It runs on a points system (1 point/drill, 1 point/AD day, up to 15 membership points/year) toward a 20-'good year' qualifying threshold, with a non-regular pension that generally starts at age 60 (reducible, not below age 50, for qualifying post-2008 mobilized duty). Applies identically to Guard/Reserve intelligence billets as to any other specialty. Note: security clearance sponsorship and drill-status access to classified systems for Guard/Reserve intelligence personnel is a real, separate administrative dependency not captured by the points system itself.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, in exchange for reduced monthly retired pay until full Social Security retirement age (~67), when the monthly amount returns to the uncut level. Fully taxable in the year received.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility (up to 180 days of permissive duty in the final window before separation) is not specialty-restricted, so 1N0X1 Airmen near separation are as eligible as any other AFSC in principle -- subject to the same real requirement as everyone else: unit commander approval, which is discretionary based on unit staffing/operational needs (data/transition.json). The cleared-contractor sector is commonly understood to be an active SkillBridge-partner space for this career field, but this was not independently verified this session -- flagged as a plausible inference, not a sourced claim.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, a VA Benefits I course, a DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track (Higher Education, Career/Technical Training, or Entrepreneurship/Boots to Business), ending in a Capstone that certifies Career Readiness Standards are met. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry decides eligibility for nearly every benefit below -- VA disability, VA healthcare, GI Bill, VA home loan, and most state veteran benefits. For this cluster specifically, the DD-214 alone does not carry classification/access information -- a separate record (JPAS/DISS eligibility history) governs whether a departing member's clearance can be reinstated or reused by a future cleared employer.
va_disability_common_claims: GUESS/INFERENCE, not sourced from a specialty-linked VA claims dataset this session (data/transition.json's specialty_linked_common_claims list covers combat arms, aviation, infantry, and PACT Act-covered theater deployment, but does NOT include an intelligence/analysis entry). Reasoned from the nature of the work rather than pulled from a VA claims-frequency source:
Vision strain and related eye conditions from years of sustained close-focus screen/console work reading imagery, signals data, and reporting

Cervical and lumbar spine/musculoskeletal strain from prolonged sedentary console/watch-floor duty (plausible by analogy to other console-heavy specialties, not independently confirmed for this cluster)

Mental-health conditions -- anxiety, depression, occupational stress, and in some cases PTSD-spectrum symptoms -- from routine, repeated exposure to disturbing raw intelligence material (ISR/strike imagery, SIGINT intercepts, HUMINT reporting of atrocities or trauma); this pattern is documented in published research on remote/ISR imagery analysts and drone-associated personnel, but was not independently sourced to a VA claims dataset for this specific cluster this session

For members who deploy forward embedded with a supported combat-arms or aviation unit rather than working from a fixed fusion center, the deployed unit's own exposure profile (noise, PACT Act-covered airborne hazards, etc.) may also apply -- see the combat-arms/aviation/PACT-Act claim families in data/transition.json.
To verify: check VA's published claims-by-occupation data (if VA publishes it) or consult a VSO familiar with intelligence MOS/rating/AFSC populations before treating this list as authoritative.

Excludes the DoD TSP contribution (up to 5% of base pay, ~$1,444/yr at max match) and TRICARE's non-cash value, neither of which pay-tables.json could quantify to primary-source confidence this session.

bonuses.conditions: No enlistment or reenlistment bonus figure specific to 0231 was found or confirmed this session. Marine Corps enlistment bonuses are published by MOS and reset quarterly; verify current 0231 eligibility and amount directly with Marine Corps Recruiting Command or a recruiter before relying on any number.

career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: General Marine Corps enlisted promotion pattern (time-in-service/time-in-grade plus a cutting-score promotion system); no 0231-specific deviation was found this session.
reenlistment_decision_points: End of initial enlistment contract (commonly 4 years)
Periodic SSBI reinvestigation -- an adverse finding is a real, MOS-specific fork
The 20-year retention decision (pension cliff -- see retirement block)
advanced_schools_or_certs: Follow-on intelligence-discipline courses at NMITC (counterintelligence, HUMINT, SIGINT, cyber intelligence, geospatial intelligence) -- confirmed to exist this session via the Coast Guard IS curriculum description at the same schoolhouse, but a Marine-specific course list was not independently confirmed
career_progression.officer_conversion_path: Commissioning via OCS or the Platoon Leaders Course (PLC) into the Intelligence Officer MOS (0202 series). Exact current prerequisites were not independently verified this session.
career_progression.source: General Marine Corps enlisted-career-progression structure; not independently fetched to primary-source confidence this session.

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years -- there is no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension for separating at 19 years or 10 years. Before 20 years, an intelligence specialist keeps every dollar of their own TSP contributions and earnings (always vested), plus DoD's automatic 1%-of-pay TSP contribution and its match (vested after 2 years of service) -- all portable into a civilian 401(k)/IRA. The defined-benefit pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years, per DoD's own language. This applies identically regardless of specialty; intelligence carries no special rule.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service (exact window and multiplier set annually per branch; Army's published active-component rate is 2.5x monthly basic pay for a 4-year additional service obligation). No intelligence-specific continuation-pay rate was identified this session -- treat the general BRS figure as the baseline and verify the current branch amount before relying on it.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. It runs on a points system (1 point/drill, 1 point/AD day, up to 15 membership points/year) toward a 20-'good year' qualifying threshold, with a non-regular pension that generally starts at age 60 (reducible, not below age 50, for qualifying post-2008 mobilized duty). Applies identically to Guard/Reserve intelligence billets as to any other specialty. Note: security clearance sponsorship and drill-status access to classified systems for Guard/Reserve intelligence personnel is a real, separate administrative dependency not captured by the points system itself.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, in exchange for reduced monthly retired pay until full Social Security retirement age (~67), when the monthly amount returns to the uncut level. Fully taxable in the year received.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility (up to 180 days of permissive duty in the final window before separation) is not specialty-restricted, so 0231 Marines near separation are as eligible as any other MOS in principle -- subject to the same real requirement as everyone else: unit commander approval, which is discretionary based on unit staffing/operational needs (data/transition.json). The cleared-contractor sector is commonly understood to be an active SkillBridge-partner space for this career field, but this was not independently verified this session -- flagged as a plausible inference, not a sourced claim.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, a VA Benefits I course, a DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track (Higher Education, Career/Technical Training, or Entrepreneurship/Boots to Business), ending in a Capstone that certifies Career Readiness Standards are met. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry decides eligibility for nearly every benefit below -- VA disability, VA healthcare, GI Bill, VA home loan, and most state veteran benefits. For this cluster specifically, the DD-214 alone does not carry classification/access information -- a separate record (JPAS/DISS eligibility history) governs whether a departing member's clearance can be reinstated or reused by a future cleared employer.
va_disability_common_claims: GUESS/INFERENCE, not sourced from a specialty-linked VA claims dataset this session (data/transition.json's specialty_linked_common_claims list covers combat arms, aviation, infantry, and PACT Act-covered theater deployment, but does NOT include an intelligence/analysis entry). Reasoned from the nature of the work rather than pulled from a VA claims-frequency source:
Vision strain and related eye conditions from years of sustained close-focus screen/console work reading imagery, signals data, and reporting

Cervical and lumbar spine/musculoskeletal strain from prolonged sedentary console/watch-floor duty (plausible by analogy to other console-heavy specialties, not independently confirmed for this cluster)

Mental-health conditions -- anxiety, depression, occupational stress, and in some cases PTSD-spectrum symptoms -- from routine, repeated exposure to disturbing raw intelligence material (ISR/strike imagery, SIGINT intercepts, HUMINT reporting of atrocities or trauma); this pattern is documented in published research on remote/ISR imagery analysts and drone-associated personnel, but was not independently sourced to a VA claims dataset for this specific cluster this session

For members who deploy forward embedded with a supported combat-arms or aviation unit rather than working from a fixed fusion center, the deployed unit's own exposure profile (noise, PACT Act-covered airborne hazards, etc.) may also apply -- see the combat-arms/aviation/PACT-Act claim families in data/transition.json.
To verify: check VA's published claims-by-occupation data (if VA publishes it) or consult a VSO familiar with intelligence MOS/rating/AFSC populations before treating this list as authoritative.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an employment handicap, filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json), drawn from a separate entitlement bucket than the Post-9/11 GI Bill. No intelligence-specific eligibility variance.

relevant_certifications: No automatic civilian license or certification is conferred by this MOS's training -- see credential_gap_note.
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: CROSSWALK NUANCE (both sides must be named): A current, in-scope TS/SCI clearance is one of the single most valuable transferable assets a service member carries out of the military -- the cleared-contractor labor market (firms supporting DoD/IC customers) pays a real, well-documented premium for candidates who already hold an active clearance, because clearance processing for a new hire can otherwise take many months to over a year. BUT two frictions are equally real and must not be glossed over: (1) a clearance LAPSES after separation -- it does not stay active indefinitely once the member leaves a billet that required it, and reinstating it typically depends on a gap-period rule (commonly cited as roughly 24 months before a full new investigation is required, though this specific window was not independently verified this session) plus a new employer willing to sponsor/re-affiliate the investigation; periodic reinvestigation (every 5 years for SCI access, per the Navy IS sourcing this session) is also required to STAY current while serving. (2) Much of the actual day-to-day work -- specific targets, sources, methods, systems, and products -- cannot be described in any detail on a civilian resume or in an interview, which is a genuine, underdiscussed transition friction: veterans from this cluster often have to learn to translate classified experience into unclassified, resume-safe competency language (analysis tradecraft, briefing/writing skill, tools/platforms where releasable) rather than simply listing what they did. For 0231 specifically: this MOS's tactical, ground-combat-embedded analysis experience is a differentiator for roles supporting DoD tactical/operational customers, but the clearance-lapse and resume-opacity frictions above still apply in full.
civilian_crosswalk.source: O*NET 33-3021.06 'Intelligence Analysts' is a detailed occupation nested under the broader BLS Standard Occupational Classification 33-3021 'Detectives and Criminal Investigators' -- BLS's Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OEWS) program does not publish wage data broken out at O*NET's more granular detailed-occupation level, only at the parent SOC code. The range below is therefore the parent SOC 33-3021 10th-90th percentile band (which also includes non-intelligence criminal investigators/detectives, so it is directionally informative rather than an exact intelligence-analyst-only figure). A federal-sector data point for context (not used as the primary range, and not independently verified to primary-source level this session): the federal Intelligence Series (GS-0132) reportedly starts entry-level analysts around GS-7 (~$55,924 in 2024 DC-locality pay per a secondary aggregator), with mid-career GG-0132-13-equivalent postings reported in the $103,409-$134,435 range -- federal/IC pay scales diverge meaningfully from the private cleared-contractor market and from the BLS occupational-category figure used below. https://www.bls.gov/oes/2023/may/oes333021.htm (BLS OEWS, SOC 33-3021 Detectives and Criminal Investigators, May 2024 reference period, $93,580 median / $48,230 10th pctl / $120,460 90th pctl, retrieved via web search this session); https://www.onetonline.org/link/summary/33-3021.06 (O*NET 33-3021.06 Intelligence Analysts detailed-occupation page, confirms the code and typical titles, nested under parent SOC 33-3021); federal GS-0132 context figures via federalpay.org and opm.gov (secondary aggregation, not primary-fetched)

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart / service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. No tier below 50%. Only qualifying Title 10 active-duty days count -- drills, AT, and initial training duty status do not. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment -- a real service commitment, not paperwork-only. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving; cannot be initiated after separation. Dependent children must wait until the member has 10+ years of service to start using it.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting (credit/income/DTI) and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3% of loan amount depending on down payment and first/subsequent use, waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure service history, income, and other VA-benefit receipt -- not by specialty. An intelligence specialist's group assignment depends entirely on their individual disability rating and income profile, not their MOS/rating/AFSC.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property-tax exemptions, income-tax treatment, tuition waivers, etc.) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure -- e.g., a 100%-disabled veteran gets a full residence-homestead property-tax exemption in Texas but a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools (in-state rate applies to all users) or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a housing allowance keyed to the school's zip code and a $1,000/yr book stipend -- scaled by the eligibility tier above. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving. Applies the same across all six branches per data/education-benefits.json; branch-specific semester-hour usage limits were not independently confirmed to primary-source level in that file.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Marine Corps Credentialing Opportunities On-Line (COOL): specific dollar cap NOT confirmed this session -- a direct fetch to cool.osd.mil failed (data/education-benefits.json notes a certificate error). (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: No civilian license is conferred automatically by this specialty's military training. The credential that matters most in this labor market is the clearance itself, not a vendor certification. Branch COOL programs may list vendor-neutral analytic-tradecraft or, for cyber-adjacent SIGINT roles, DoD 8140-baseline certifications (e.g., CompTIA Security+) as fundable via Credentialing Assistance -- this was NOT independently confirmed against cool.osd.mil for this specific MOS/rating/AFSC this session (this project's education-benefits.json notes repeated certificate-error failures fetching cool.osd.mil); verify current COOL credential listings for this specific code before publishing a specific certification claim.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: 0231 Marines deploy embedded with the supported Marine Air-Ground Task Force's intelligence section, and staff garrison intelligence shops between deployments. No sourced deployment-frequency figure specific to 0231 was found this session; tempo generally follows the supported unit's operational tempo.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Garrison assignments are relatively family-stable compared to forward-deployed MAGTF tours, which follow the supported unit's schedule and can involve sustained separation.

entry_requirements.education: High school diploma generally expected (inherits the Air Force accession pipeline per data/branch-profiles.json).
entry_requirements.age_range: No Space Force-specific enlistment age minimum/maximum is separately published; the service inherits the Air Force's enlisted accession pipeline and reported age ceiling (up to 42 per secondary sources) -- per data/branch-profiles.json, not independently re-confirmed this session.
entry_requirements.physical: Standard DoD/Air-Force-shared accession medical and fitness standards (Space Force shares BMT with the Air Force). No specialty-specific elevated standard was found.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: TS/SCI is highly likely to be required given the intelligence function and this cluster's pattern across every other branch, but this was NOT independently confirmed for a specific Space Force intelligence specialty this session -- the official spaceforce.mil enlisted specialty crosswalk PDF returned HTTP 403 on fetch this session, consistent with data/interest-taxonomy.json's prior finding on the same document.
entry_requirements.other: Under DoD's 13 Adjudicative Guidelines (Security Executive Agent Directive 4 / 32 CFR Part 147), none of the following are automatic mechanical disqualifiers under the 'whole person' standard, but they are the dominant real-world reasons applicants and incumbents are denied or lose eligibility, and each can rule out this specialty even with a fully qualifying ASVAB score: (1) unresolved foreign contacts, foreign relatives in certain countries, foreign property/financial interests, or foreign romantic ties that create potential foreign leverage (Guideline B, Foreign Influence); (2) a pattern of serious financial delinquency -- unresolved debt (especially over $10,000), recent bankruptcy, wage garnishment, or repeated late payment patterns (Guideline F; financial issues are cited as roughly 45% of DoD clearance denials); (3) illegal drug use, misuse of prescription medication, or continued use after applying (Guideline H); and (4), treated especially seriously, deliberately concealing or falsifying any of the above -- including an UNREPORTED dual citizenship or undisclosed foreign contact -- during the application or investigation (Guideline E, Personal Conduct). A 16-24-year-old considering this cluster should treat clearance eligibility as a real, independent requirement, not a formality layered on top of a qualifying ASVAB score. Given the Space Force's very small total enlisted force and correspondingly small number of intelligence billets, competition for available slots is reported to be higher than in the equivalent Air Force career field, though this was not independently sourced this session.

veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property-tax exemptions, income-tax treatment, tuition waivers, etc.) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure -- e.g., a 100%-disabled veteran gets a full residence-homestead property-tax exemption in Texas but a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools (in-state rate applies to all users) or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a housing allowance keyed to the school's zip code and a $1,000/yr book stipend -- scaled by the eligibility tier above. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving. Applies the same across all six branches per data/education-benefits.json; branch-specific semester-hour usage limits were not independently confirmed to primary-source level in that file.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: UNVERIFIED. Assumed to follow the same Department of the Air Force AFVEC-administered Credentialing Assistance program and cap ($4,500/year) as the Air Force, since Space Force falls under the Department of the Air Force -- this is an inference from data/education-benefits.json, not a directly sourced figure. Confirm before publishing.
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: No civilian license is conferred automatically by this specialty's military training. The credential that matters most in this labor market is the clearance itself, not a vendor certification. Branch COOL programs may list vendor-neutral analytic-tradecraft or, for cyber-adjacent SIGINT roles, DoD 8140-baseline certifications (e.g., CompTIA Security+) as fundable via Credentialing Assistance -- this was NOT independently confirmed against cool.osd.mil for this specific MOS/rating/AFSC this session (this project's education-benefits.json notes repeated certificate-error failures fetching cool.osd.mil); verify current COOL credential listings for this specific code before publishing a specific certification claim.

entry_requirements.education: High school diploma or GED (Coast Guard general accession standard, AFQT minimum 32 per data/branch-profiles.json/prep-pathway.md); no IS-specific deviation found.

entry_requirements.security_clearance: UNVERIFIED at the specific-tier level (Secret vs. TS/SCI) this session. The IS rating page fetched this session describes the role as requiring personnel who are 'comfortable keeping secrets' and whose training includes 'protecting classified information,' consistent with this cluster's pattern across every other branch (TS/SCI, SSBI-adjudicated), but the fetched page did not state an explicit clearance tier -- treat TS/SCI as the working assumption pending direct recruiter confirmation.
entry_requirements.other: Under DoD's 13 Adjudicative Guidelines (Security Executive Agent Directive 4 / 32 CFR Part 147), none of the following are automatic mechanical disqualifiers under the 'whole person' standard, but they are the dominant real-world reasons applicants and incumbents are denied or lose eligibility, and each can rule out this specialty even with a fully qualifying ASVAB score: (1) unresolved foreign contacts, foreign relatives in certain countries, foreign property/financial interests, or foreign romantic ties that create potential foreign leverage (Guideline B, Foreign Influence); (2) a pattern of serious financial delinquency -- unresolved debt (especially over $10,000), recent bankruptcy, wage garnishment, or repeated late payment patterns (Guideline F; financial issues are cited as roughly 45% of DoD clearance denials); (3) illegal drug use, misuse of prescription medication, or continued use after applying (Guideline H); and (4), treated especially seriously, deliberately concealing or falsifying any of the above -- including an UNREPORTED dual citizenship or undisclosed foreign contact -- during the application or investigation (Guideline E, Personal Conduct). A 16-24-year-old considering this cluster should treat clearance eligibility as a real, independent requirement, not a formality layered on top of a qualifying ASVAB score.

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years -- there is no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension for separating at 19 years or 10 years. Before 20 years, an intelligence specialist keeps every dollar of their own TSP contributions and earnings (always vested), plus DoD's automatic 1%-of-pay TSP contribution and its match (vested after 2 years of service) -- all portable into a civilian 401(k)/IRA. The defined-benefit pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years, per DoD's own language. This applies identically regardless of specialty; intelligence carries no special rule.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service (exact window and multiplier set annually per branch; Army's published active-component rate is 2.5x monthly basic pay for a 4-year additional service obligation). No intelligence-specific continuation-pay rate was identified this session -- treat the general BRS figure as the baseline and verify the current branch amount before relying on it.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. It runs on a points system (1 point/drill, 1 point/AD day, up to 15 membership points/year) toward a 20-'good year' qualifying threshold, with a non-regular pension that generally starts at age 60 (reducible, not below age 50, for qualifying post-2008 mobilized duty). Applies identically to Guard/Reserve intelligence billets as to any other specialty. Note: security clearance sponsorship and drill-status access to classified systems for Guard/Reserve intelligence personnel is a real, separate administrative dependency not captured by the points system itself.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, in exchange for reduced monthly retired pay until full Social Security retirement age (~67), when the monthly amount returns to the uncut level. Fully taxable in the year received.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility is not specialty-restricted in principle, but data/transition.json flags that Coast Guard-specific SkillBridge/TAP administration (the Coast Guard is a DHS component, not DoD) was NOT independently verified in this project's TRANSITION research pass -- DoD-wide policy was applied there and may differ in Coast Guard-specific instructions. Unit commander approval remains the real, discretionary requirement wherever it applies.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, a VA Benefits I course, a DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track (Higher Education, Career/Technical Training, or Entrepreneurship/Boots to Business), ending in a Capstone that certifies Career Readiness Standards are met. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry decides eligibility for nearly every benefit below -- VA disability, VA healthcare, GI Bill, VA home loan, and most state veteran benefits. For this cluster specifically, the DD-214 alone does not carry classification/access information -- a separate record (JPAS/DISS eligibility history) governs whether a departing member's clearance can be reinstated or reused by a future cleared employer.
va_disability_common_claims: GUESS/INFERENCE, not sourced from a specialty-linked VA claims dataset this session (data/transition.json's specialty_linked_common_claims list covers combat arms, aviation, infantry, and PACT Act-covered theater deployment, but does NOT include an intelligence/analysis entry). Reasoned from the nature of the work rather than pulled from a VA claims-frequency source:
Vision strain and related eye conditions from years of sustained close-focus screen/console work reading imagery, signals data, and reporting

Cervical and lumbar spine/musculoskeletal strain from prolonged sedentary console/watch-floor duty (plausible by analogy to other console-heavy specialties, not independently confirmed for this cluster)

Mental-health conditions -- anxiety, depression, occupational stress, and in some cases PTSD-spectrum symptoms -- from routine, repeated exposure to disturbing raw intelligence material (ISR/strike imagery, SIGINT intercepts, HUMINT reporting of atrocities or trauma); this pattern is documented in published research on remote/ISR imagery analysts and drone-associated personnel, but was not independently sourced to a VA claims dataset for this specific cluster this session

For members who deploy forward embedded with a supported combat-arms or aviation unit rather than working from a fixed fusion center, the deployed unit's own exposure profile (noise, PACT Act-covered airborne hazards, etc.) may also apply -- see the combat-arms/aviation/PACT-Act claim families in data/transition.json.
To verify: check VA's published claims-by-occupation data (if VA publishes it) or consult a VSO familiar with intelligence MOS/rating/AFSC populations before treating this list as authoritative.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an employment handicap, filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json), drawn from a separate entitlement bucket than the Post-9/11 GI Bill. No intelligence-specific eligibility variance.

relevant_certifications: No automatic civilian license or certification is conferred by this rating's training -- see credential_gap_note.
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: CROSSWALK NUANCE (both sides must be named): A current, in-scope TS/SCI clearance is one of the single most valuable transferable assets a service member carries out of the military -- the cleared-contractor labor market (firms supporting DoD/IC customers) pays a real, well-documented premium for candidates who already hold an active clearance, because clearance processing for a new hire can otherwise take many months to over a year. BUT two frictions are equally real and must not be glossed over: (1) a clearance LAPSES after separation -- it does not stay active indefinitely once the member leaves a billet that required it, and reinstating it typically depends on a gap-period rule (commonly cited as roughly 24 months before a full new investigation is required, though this specific window was not independently verified this session) plus a new employer willing to sponsor/re-affiliate the investigation; periodic reinvestigation (every 5 years for SCI access, per the Navy IS sourcing this session) is also required to STAY current while serving. (2) Much of the actual day-to-day work -- specific targets, sources, methods, systems, and products -- cannot be described in any detail on a civilian resume or in an interview, which is a genuine, underdiscussed transition friction: veterans from this cluster often have to learn to translate classified experience into unclassified, resume-safe competency language (analysis tradecraft, briefing/writing skill, tools/platforms where releasable) rather than simply listing what they did. For Coast Guard IS specifically: maritime-domain and homeland-security-relevant analysis experience is a differentiator for DHS-adjacent civilian and contractor roles (e.g., supporting CBP, ICE, or DHS intelligence customers), but the clearance-lapse and resume-opacity frictions above still apply in full, and Coast Guard clearance sponsorship/administration sits under DHS rather than DoD, which can affect reciprocity timelines when moving to a DoD-sponsoring employer -- this specific reciprocity point was not independently verified this session.
civilian_crosswalk.source: O*NET 33-3021.06 'Intelligence Analysts' is a detailed occupation nested under the broader BLS Standard Occupational Classification 33-3021 'Detectives and Criminal Investigators' -- BLS's Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OEWS) program does not publish wage data broken out at O*NET's more granular detailed-occupation level, only at the parent SOC code. The range below is therefore the parent SOC 33-3021 10th-90th percentile band (which also includes non-intelligence criminal investigators/detectives, so it is directionally informative rather than an exact intelligence-analyst-only figure). A federal-sector data point for context (not used as the primary range, and not independently verified to primary-source level this session): the federal Intelligence Series (GS-0132) reportedly starts entry-level analysts around GS-7 (~$55,924 in 2024 DC-locality pay per a secondary aggregator), with mid-career GG-0132-13-equivalent postings reported in the $103,409-$134,435 range -- federal/IC pay scales diverge meaningfully from the private cleared-contractor market and from the BLS occupational-category figure used below. https://www.bls.gov/oes/2023/may/oes333021.htm (BLS OEWS, SOC 33-3021 Detectives and Criminal Investigators, May 2024 reference period, $93,580 median / $48,230 10th pctl / $120,460 90th pctl, retrieved via web search this session); https://www.onetonline.org/link/summary/33-3021.06 (O*NET 33-3021.06 Intelligence Analysts detailed-occupation page, confirms the code and typical titles, nested under parent SOC 33-3021); federal GS-0132 context figures via federalpay.org and opm.gov (secondary aggregation, not primary-fetched)

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart / service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. No tier below 50%. Only qualifying Title 10 active-duty days count -- drills, AT, and initial training duty status do not. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment -- a real service commitment, not paperwork-only. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving; cannot be initiated after separation. Dependent children must wait until the member has 10+ years of service to start using it.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting (credit/income/DTI) and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3% of loan amount depending on down payment and first/subsequent use, waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure service history, income, and other VA-benefit receipt -- not by specialty. An intelligence specialist's group assignment depends entirely on their individual disability rating and income profile, not their MOS/rating/AFSC.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property-tax exemptions, income-tax treatment, tuition waivers, etc.) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure -- e.g., a 100%-disabled veteran gets a full residence-homestead property-tax exemption in Texas but a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools (in-state rate applies to all users) or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a housing allowance keyed to the school's zip code and a $1,000/yr book stipend -- scaled by the eligibility tier above. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving. Applies the same across all six branches per data/education-benefits.json; branch-specific semester-hour usage limits were not independently confirmed to primary-source level in that file.

A search summary attributed a $4,500/year figure to the Coast Guard's credentialing program, but data/education-benefits.json flags this as a likely conflation with Coast Guard Tuition Assistance rather than Credentialing Assistance specifically; direct fetch to cool.osd.mil failed this session and in the prior EDU-BENEFITS pass. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: No civilian license is conferred automatically by this specialty's military training. The credential that matters most in this labor market is the clearance itself, not a vendor certification. Branch COOL programs may list vendor-neutral analytic-tradecraft or, for cyber-adjacent SIGINT roles, DoD 8140-baseline certifications (e.g., CompTIA Security+) as fundable via Credentialing Assistance -- this was NOT independently confirmed against cool.osd.mil for this specific MOS/rating/AFSC this session (this project's education-benefits.json notes repeated certificate-error failures fetching cool.osd.mil); verify current COOL credential listings for this specific code before publishing a specific certification claim.

completion_accountability: 6 of 6 branches represented, one flagship enlisted specialty record per branch (Army 35F, Navy IS, Air Force 1N0X1, Marine Corps 0231, Space Force intelligence [unverified code], Coast Guard IS), within the 5-7 record target. 0 branches skipped; 0 records dropped. No branch_logo used a seal; all six branch_logo_id values were taken from the existing data/asset-manifest.json AssetRecord ids rather than invented. specialty_badge_id is null in every record per instruction; dvids_search_terms are supplied for all six. Tool usage: 11 web calls (10 WebSearch + 1 WebFetch) against the ~10-call budget guideline -- 1 call over, spent on confirming a real BLS wage figure (SOC 33-3021) rather than fabricating or omitting the estimated_civilian_salary_range_usd field, which is non-nullable in the schema and recurs in all 6 records. No .mil domain was fetched directly except gocoastguard.com (which succeeded); no call was spent re-chasing a domain already known from this project's prior sessions to return HTTP 403 (militarypay.defense.gov, dfas.mil, travel.dod.mil, tsp.gov, spaceforce.mil, cool.osd.mil, mynavyhr.navy.mil, static.e-publishing.af.mil, api.army.mil, intelligence.marines.mil were all treated as search-only per the budget instruction to not burn calls chasing 403s).
